chunk_summary: Guidance on hydration counseling for well children, emphasizing age-specific water needs, beverage selection, and monitoring cues for adequate hydration.
learning_objectives: Understand age-specific total water intake recommendations for children based on Dietary Reference Intakes (DRI).
Learn appropriate beverage choices for routine hydration in children, including water, milk, and unsweetened fluids.
Recognize signs of dehydration and implement monitoring strategies for hydration adequacy.
Avoid harmful practices such as excessive sweetened beverage consumption or inappropriate water introduction in infants.
key_facts: Total water AI targets: 1.3 L/day (1–3 years), 1.7 L/day (4–8 years), 2.1 L/day (girls 9–13 years), 2.4 L/day (boys 9–13 years), 2.3 L/day (girls 14–18 years), 3.3 L/day (boys 14–18 years).
Foods contribute roughly one-third of total water intake; beverage volumes are lower than AI targets.
Infants 0–6 months should not receive extra water; breastmilk/formula suffices for hydration needs.
Signs of adequate hydration: regular daytime voiding and pale-yellow urine; dehydration signs include dark urine, lethargy, or no urine for prolonged intervals.
Avoid sugar-sweetened beverages and energy drinks for routine hydration; prioritize water and nutrient beverages like milk within age-appropriate volumes.
reference_quote: DRI tables provide AI targets for total water in temperate conditions: 1–3 years about 1.3 L/day, 4–8 years about 1.7 L/day, 9–13 years about 2.1 L/day (girls) and 2.4 L/day (boys), and 14–18 years about 2.3 L/day (girls) and 3.3 L/day (boys).
